text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 2 December 2017 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy following left temporal lobe insult (aetiology under investigation)/generalised anxiety symptoms/insomnia/mild memory difficulties/smoker. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily.  
Sertraline 50 mg once daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily.  
Vitamin D3 800 IU once daily.  

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today, accompanied by his partner who has kept a detailed seizure diary. He reports improved daytime alertness since the lamotrigine dose was optimised in September, with fewer post-ictal headaches. He continues to work part-time as a delivery coordinator and has recently resumed light exercise. There have been no injuries, hospital attendances, or missed doses reported. Sleep remains fragmented, with early-morning awakenings two to three times per week, which he associates with worry about seizure occurrence. 

Focal epilepsy: He describes typical focal aware seizures featuring a rising epigastric sensation, brief speech arrest, and a metallic taste, lasting under a minute, sometimes progressing to focal impaired awareness with automatisms and brief confusion. There is no history of generalised tonic–clonic seizures this year. Triggers reported include sleep deprivation and high stress. 

Seizures: Based on the diary and partner report, the pattern this year has been as follows: Seizures in 2017-2017: May: 8 days with seizures June: 1 days with seizures July: 9 days with more severe seizures August: 7 days with more severe seizures, September: 12 days with seizures October: 3 days, most of them at sleep time, November: 7 days. The more severe days in July and August corresponded to clusters of focal impaired awareness events, whereas October events largely occurred during sleep with brief confusion on waking. 

Investigations: MRI brain (May 2017) shows subtle left mesial temporal volume loss without signal change. Routine EEG (June 2017) demonstrated left temporal sharp waves. Bloods in October 2017: FBC, U&E, LFTs within reference; vitamin D borderline low (repletion ongoing). Lamotrigine level within therapeutic range; lacosamide level not measured. 

Driving: He is currently not driving and is aware of DVLA regulations; he confirms ongoing self-notification and compliance. 

Plan:  
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and lacosamide 100 mg twice daily with adherence reinforced.  
- Sleep hygiene reviewed; consider short-term melatonin via GP if insomnia persists (patient-led choice).  
- Arrange 48–72 hr ambulatory EEG to characterise nocturnal events.  
- Repeat vitamin D level in 3 months via GP.  
- Safety advice reiterated regarding bathing, heights, and operating machinery.  
- Follow-up in 4 months or sooner if clusters recur.  

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter lists monthly counts of 'days with seizures' for May through November 2017. Summing these: May 8 + June 1 + July 9 + August 7 + September 12 + October 3 + November 7 = 47 seizure days. The observation window spans 7 months (May to November inclusive). Therefore, the aggregate frequency is 47 per 7 months.
